summary: This PR adds support for generator-based WSGI applications by introducing a `_get_non_empty_chunk` helper that skips leading empty chunks before the response status/headers are asserted. While the core approach works for the common case, the review surfaces several correctness gaps (most notably around PEP 3333 compliance for middleware-style generators and missing `close()` calls), security concerns (exception leakage, DoS via infinite empty generators), and a number of style/coverage issues. The most critical issues are the latent correctness gap when `start_response` is called after a yield and the missing `close()` call on the original WSGI iterator.

findings.title: Generator apps that call start_response after first yield may trigger AssertionError
findings.detail: When a WSGI application is a generator function, `self.app(environ, start_response)` at line 100 merely creates a generator object — no code in the function body executes until the generator is iterated. `_get_non_empty_chunk` (lines 11–17) drives that iteration, so `start_response` is only invoked during iteration inside the helper. This works for the common case (start_response called before the first yield), but PEP 3333 explicitly permits middleware-style generators to call `start_response` *after* an initial yield. In that case, `_get_non_empty_chunk` may exhaust the iterator or return before `start_response` has been called, causing the assertions at lines 105–106 (`seen_status is not None`) to raise `AssertionError` instead of producing a valid response. The helper should also be documented or enforced to guarantee that `start_response` has been invoked upon return, or the assertion placement should be reconsidered.

findings.title: close() not called on original WSGI result iterator (PEP 3333 violation)
findings.detail: PEP 3333 requires that if the iterable returned by the WSGI application has a `close()` method, the server must call it after the response is finished. After wrapping with `itertools.chain([chunk], body)`, the `close()` method of the original iterator object is lost and will never be called, potentially leaking file handles or other resources in WSGI apps that rely on `close()` for cleanup. The original `result` object's `close()` should be called in a `finally` block after iteration, or the chain wrapper should proxy `close()` to the underlying iterator.

findings.title: Exceptions raised inside a WSGI generator bypass the raise_app_exceptions guard
findings.detail: The PR changes the iterator consumption so that `_get_non_empty_chunk` eagerly iterates chunks before the `seen_exc_info` / `raise_app_exceptions` check. If the WSGI app raises inside its generator body during this eager iteration, the exception propagates unconditionally and bypasses the `raise_app_exceptions=False` guard that callers may depend on for suppression. An attacker-controlled or buggy WSGI app in a reverse-proxy scenario could use this to surface raw Python tracebacks to callers who do not expect them.

findings.title: HTTP header injection via unsanitised request header names written into environ
findings.detail: At lines 82–86, incoming request header names are uppercased and written directly into the WSGI `environ` dict with no sanitisation. A client can supply a header whose normalised key collides with a security-sensitive environ key (e.g. a header named `Server-Port` overwriting an already-set environ value). Only `CONTENT_TYPE` and `CONTENT_LENGTH` are excluded from the `HTTP_` prefix path; any other CGI-like key can be injected. While this is pre-existing behaviour, the PR adds no mitigation. At minimum, header names should be validated to contain only ASCII letters, digits, and hyphens before insertion.

findings.title: Misleading function name: _get_non_empty_chunk does not return a single chunk
findings.detail: `_get_non_empty_chunk` implies it returns one chunk, but it actually returns the full remaining iterable (with the first non-empty chunk prepended via `itertools.chain`). A name like `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` or `_iter_from_first_non_empty` would be more accurate and consistent with how iterator helpers are named elsewhere in the codebase.

findings.title: Unbounded iteration over empty chunks enables denial-of-service
findings.detail: `_get_non_empty_chunk` iterates unconditionally through all empty chunks before finding a non-empty one. A malicious or buggy WSGI application that returns an infinite generator of empty byte strings (`b''`) will cause this function to loop forever, resulting in a CPU-bound denial-of-service. There is no cap on the number of empty chunks consumed. Consider adding a maximum number of empty chunks that may be skipped before aborting with an error.

findings.title: application_factory name does not communicate what the application does
findings.detail: Existing test helpers (`echo_body`, `echo_body_with_response_stream`, `raise_exc`) are named after the application's behaviour, not its construction mechanism. `application_factory` is a generic structural name that makes tests harder to read at a glance. Splitting concerns — keeping a dedicated `hello_world` function using `yield`, and adding a separate generator-based helper only where needed — would be more consistent with the existing style.

findings.title: Missing tests for list-based iterables with empty chunks and for truly empty iterables
findings.detail: The new `_get_non_empty_chunk` helper is exercised by generator-based test apps, but two important paths are untested: (1) a traditional return-based WSGI app that returns a plain list with leading empty chunks (e.g. `[b'', b'hello']`) is never tested; (2) a WSGI app that returns a completely empty iterable (`[]`, zero items) is never tested — only the all-empty-chunks case (`[b'', b'', ...]`) is covered by `test_wsgi_generator_empty`. Both paths should have explicit tests.

findings.title: Header propagation after _get_non_empty_chunk is only implicitly covered
findings.detail: No test explicitly asserts that status and headers are correctly propagated after `_get_non_empty_chunk` processes a non-generator (return-based) WSGI app's iterable. The existing `echo_body` and `echo_body_with_response_stream` tests exercise this path implicitly, but none of them focus on verifying the status/header values after the helper runs. Adding an explicit assertion would provide clearer regression protection.

findings.title: Overly broad type annotation: Iterable should be Iterable[bytes]
findings.detail: The `body` parameter and return type are annotated as the bare `typing.Iterable` generic. The existing code throughout this file uses fully-specified generics such as `typing.Iterable[bytes]`. Narrowing to `typing.Iterable[bytes]` would be consistent with the surrounding style and improve type-checker accuracy.

findings.title: Docstring is too terse and partially inaccurate
findings.detail: The current docstring "Get a non-empty chunk from body" describes only part of what the function does; it also returns the rest of the iterator. Something like "Advance `body` past any leading empty chunks and return the remaining iterator, or an empty list if all chunks are empty." would be clearer and more consistent with the explanatory style of other docstrings in this file.

findings.title: Inline comment uses 'served' where 'yielded' or 'returned' is more precise
findings.detail: The inline comment reads "shouldn't be used until the first non-empty chunk has been served" — "served" is unusual in this context. "yielded" or "returned" would be clearer and more consistent with the spec language used elsewhere in the file.

findings.title: Redundant assertion: response.text and response.content check the same payload
findings.detail: Checking both `response.text == "Some content and more content"` and `response.content == b"Some content and more content"` is redundant — existing tests only assert `response.text`. Either pick one assertion or add a comment explaining why both are intentionally checked.

findings.title: start_response not called before assertions when app is a generator function
findings.detail: When the WSGI app is a generator function (uses `yield`), calling `self.app(environ, start_response)` at line 100 only creates a generator object — it does not execute any of the function body, including the `start_response(...)` call. `_get_non_empty_chunk` iterates the generator, which does trigger `start_response`, so by line 105 `seen_status` is set in the non-empty-body case. However, in the all-empty-chunks case (`test_wsgi_generator_empty`), `_get_non_empty_chunk` exhausts the generator and returns `[]` — but critically, for a generator function that calls `start_response` **before** any `yield`, iterating to exhaustion still invokes `start_response`, so the assertions pass. This is safe only because `start_response` precedes all `yield` statements in `application_factory`. But the broader correctness issue is: if a conformant WSGI generator app calls `start_response` **after** the first `yield` (which PEP 3333 permits for middleware), then after `_get_non_empty_chunk` the assertions at lines 105–106 will fail with `AssertionError` rather than producing a valid response. This is a latent correctness gap introduced by this PR.

findings.title: _get_non_empty_chunk silently drops all-empty-chunk bodies without guaranteeing start_response was called
findings.detail: When the iterable contains only empty bytestrings (e.g. `[b'', b'', b'']`), `_get_non_empty_chunk` exhausts the iterator and returns `[]`. For a non-generator WSGI app, `start_response` has already been called before `self.app()` returns, so the assertions at lines 105-106 hold. But for a generator-based app where `start_response` is called inside the generator body, the only way it gets called is during iteration inside `_get_non_empty_chunk`. If the generator yields only empty chunks interleaved with a `start_response` call that itself depends on some yield point (valid per PEP 3333 for middleware), the current code may assert `seen_status is not None` on a `None` value. The function should document or enforce that it guarantees `start_response` has been invoked on return.

findings.title: close() not called on original WSGI result iterator
findings.detail: PEP 3333 requires that if the iterable returned by the WSGI application has a `close()` method, the server must call it when the response is finished. After wrapping with `itertools.chain([chunk], body)`, the `close()` method of the original iterator is lost and will never be called, potentially leaking resources in WSGI apps that rely on this for cleanup. The original `result` object's `close()` should be called after iteration, or the chain wrapper should proxy `close()` to the underlying iterator.

findings.title: HTTP header injection via unsanitised request header names written into environ
findings.detail: At lines 82-86, every incoming request header is uppercased and written directly into the WSGI `environ` dict with no sanitisation of the header name itself. A header name containing a newline or other special character is not stripped before the `key.upper().replace('-', '_')` transform; more critically, nothing stops a client from supplying a header whose normalised key collides with a security-sensitive environ key such as `HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR` or – in the worst case – something like `SCRIPT_NAME` or `SERVER_NAME` through a specially crafted header. While the non-HTTP_ keys are in an exclusion list, only `CONTENT_TYPE` and `CONTENT_LENGTH` are excluded; any other CGI-like key (e.g. a header called `Server-Port`) can overwrite an already-set environ value. This was pre-existing, but this PR adds no mitigation. Recommend at minimum rejecting header names that contain characters other than ASCII letters, digits, and hyphens before they are inserted.

findings.title: WSGI app exception re-raised without sanitisation may leak internal exception objects
findings.detail: At line 108, `seen_exc_info[1]` (the raw exception object from the WSGI app's `exc_info` tuple) is re-raised directly. This was pre-existing, but the PR changes how the iterator is consumed (line 103) which means `_get_non_empty_chunk` will now eagerly consume chunks before the `seen_exc_info` check. If the WSGI app raises inside its generator during `_get_non_empty_chunk`, the exception bypasses the `raise_app_exceptions` guard entirely and propagates unconditionally. An attacker-controlled WSGI app (e.g. in a reverse-proxy scenario) could use this to surface internal Python tracebacks to callers who set `raise_app_exceptions=False` expecting suppression.

findings.title: Unbounded iteration over attacker-influenced WSGI generator in _get_non_empty_chunk
findings.detail: `_get_non_empty_chunk` iterates unconditionally through all empty chunks before it finds a non-empty one. A malicious or buggy WSGI application that returns an infinite generator of empty byte strings (`b''`) will cause this function to loop forever, resulting in a denial-of-service (infinite CPU loop / hung request). There is no limit on the number of empty chunks consumed. Consider adding a maximum number of empty chunks that will be skipped before aborting.

findings.title: Function name doesn't match its actual behaviour
findings.detail: `_get_non_empty_chunk` sounds like it returns a single chunk, but it actually returns the full remaining iterable (with the first non-empty chunk prepended). A name like `_skip_leading_empty_chunks` or `_iter_from_first_non_empty` would be more accurate and consistent with how the rest of the codebase names iterator helpers.

findings.title: Overly broad type annotation on `body` parameter
findings.detail: The parameter and return type are both annotated as `typing.Iterable` (the bare generic), while the existing code throughout the file uses fully-specified generics such as `typing.Iterable[bytes]`. Narrowing to `typing.Iterable[bytes]` would be consistent with the surrounding style.

findings.title: Docstring is too terse and slightly inaccurate
findings.detail: "Get a non-empty chunk from body" describes only part of what the function does; it also returns the rest of the iterator. The existing docstrings in the file (e.g., the class docstring) are more explanatory. Something like "Advance `body` past any leading empty chunks and return the remaining iterator, or an empty list if all chunks are empty." would be clearer.

findings.title: Inline comment phrasing is slightly awkward
findings.detail: "shouldn't be used until the first non-empty chunk has been served" — "served" is unusual here; "yielded" or "returned" would be clearer and more consistent with the spec language used elsewhere in the file.

findings.title: `application_factory` replaces the previously standalone `hello_world` fixture but loses its self-describing name
findings.detail: The existing test helpers (`echo_body`, `echo_body_with_response_stream`, `raise_exc`) are named after what the application does, not how it is constructed. `application_factory` is a generic, structural name that doesn't communicate the purpose of the returned application. Splitting the concerns — keeping a dedicated `hello_world` function using `yield` directly, and adding a separate generator-based helper only where needed — would be more consistent with the existing style.

findings.title: Redundant assertion: `response.text` and `response.content` both assert the same decoded payload
findings.detail: Checking both `response.text == "Some content and more content"` and `response.content == b"Some content and more content"` is redundant — the existing tests (`test_wsgi`, `test_wsgi_upload`, etc.) assert only `response.text`. Either pick one or add a brief comment explaining why both are checked.

findings.title: No test for a non-generator (list-based) iterable with leading empty chunks
findings.detail: The new `_get_non_empty_chunk` helper is exercised by `test_wsgi_generator` (generator with leading empty bytes) and `test_wsgi_generator_empty` (all-empty generator), but there is no test that passes a plain list (not a generator/yield-based app) with leading empty chunks through `WSGIDispatch`. The `application_factory` helper always produces a generator (`yield`), so the list-return path of `_get_non_empty_chunk` (via an app that `return`s a list instead of yielding) is only implicitly covered by `test_wsgi` with a single non-empty element. A list with leading empty bytes — e.g. `[b'', b'hello']` from a traditional return-based WSGI app — is never tested.

findings.title: No test for a single non-empty chunk with no leading empty chunks (fast path)
findings.detail: `test_wsgi` now uses `application_factory([b'Hello, World!'])` which is a generator that yields exactly one non-empty chunk. This does exercise `_get_non_empty_chunk`, but only the case where the very first chunk is non-empty. The existing `echo_body` and `echo_body_with_response_stream` tests still use non-generator apps whose iterables pass through `_get_non_empty_chunk` as well, but none of these explicitly assert the status/headers are correctly propagated after the helper processes them. This is low-risk but leaves the header-propagation path from `_get_non_empty_chunk` implicitly rather than explicitly covered.

findings.title: No test covering `_get_non_empty_chunk` applied to an app that returns an empty iterable (zero items, not all-empty items)
findings.detail: `_get_non_empty_chunk` returns `[]` when the input iterator is exhausted without finding a non-empty chunk. `test_wsgi_generator_empty` covers the case of an all-empty list (`[b'', b'', b'', b'']`). However, there is no test where the WSGI app returns a completely empty iterable (`[]` / no items at all). The `for chunk in body` loop in `_get_non_empty_chunk` would immediately fall through and return `[]`, but this path is never exercised by a test.

findings.title: Generator apps that call start_response after first yield may trigger AssertionError
findings.detail: When a WSGI application is a generator function, `self.app(environ, start_response)` at line 100 merely creates a generator object — no code in the function body executes until the generator is iterated. `_get_non_empty_chunk` (lines 11–17) drives that iteration, so `start_response` is only invoked during iteration inside the helper. findings.detail: PEP 3333 requires that if the iterable returned by the WSGI application has a `close()` method, the server must call it after the response is finished. After wrapping with `itertools.chain([chunk], body)`, the `close()` method of the original iterator object is lost and will never be called, potentially leaking file handles or other resources in WSGI apps that rely on `close()` for cleanup.
